title: Cashew nut yields
subtitle: Yields are measured in tonnes per hectare.
caption:
Cashew nut yields, 1980 to 2024

Yields are measured in tonnes per hectare.

The line chart tracks cashew nut yields for Peru, Mali, China, and Mexico from 1980 to 2024, with the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 7 tonnes per hectare. Peru’s data, shown in brown, begins around 1988 near 5.3 tonnes, fluctuates between roughly 3.7 and 6.8 tonnes through the 1990s and 2000s, peaks near 6.8 around 2005–2007, dips to about 4.4 by 2015, then rises again to over 6 tonnes around 2021 before falling to approximately 5.3 in 2024. Mali’s green line starts near 1.3 tonnes in 1992, remains flat until 2005, then climbs steadily to about 1.7 by 2010, accelerates after 2015 to reach 4.2 tonnes around 2019, then drops sharply to under 1 tonne by 2024. China’s red line begins below 0.5 tonnes in 1985, spikes to 1.5 around 1989, falls back below 0.5 by 1992, stays low until 2008, rises to 1.8 around 2013, then declines to hover near 1.1 from 2016 onward. Mexico’s blue line starts at 1.1 tonnes in 1980, surges to 6.1 in 1982, then falls to 2.5 by 1985, fluctuates between 2.3 and 3.5 until 2002, dips to 1.2 in 2003, rebounds to 3.1 in 2004, then generally declines after 2010 to end below 0.8 tonnes in 2024. All four countries show substantial year-to-year variation, with no consistent long-term upward or downward trend across the entire period for any single country.
